explanations: HIGH SCHOOL: A secondary school for teenagers.
SCHOOL BUS: A bus that takes students to and from school.
BUS STOP: A place where buses pick up or drop off passengers.
STOP SIGN: A road sign that tells drivers to stop.
SIGN LANGUAGE: A visual language using hands, face, and body movements.
LANGUAGE BARRIER: A communication problem caused by different languages.
BARRIER REEF: A long reef that runs near and parallel to a coast.
REEF FISH: A fish that lives around coral reefs.
WATER FILTER: A device that removes impurities from water.
FILTER PAPER: Paper used to filter liquids or air.
PAPER BAG: A bag made of paper.
BAG LUNCH: A packed lunch carried in a bag.
LUNCH BOX: A container used to carry lunch.
BOX TOP: The top part of a box.
TOP HAT: A tall formal hat.
HAT TRICK: Three successes by one player or person in a single event.
SUN SCREEN: A lotion that protects skin from the sun.
SCREEN DOOR: A door with mesh that lets air in and keeps insects out.
DOOR BELL: A bell or button used to announce someone at the door.
BELL TOWER: A tower that contains one or more bells.
TOWER BRIDGE: A bridge with towers, often used as a landmark name.
BRIDGE LOAN: A short-term loan used until longer-term financing is ready.
LOAN SHARK: A person who lends money at unfairly high interest.
SHARK TANK: A tank or enclosure used to display sharks.
COFFEE TABLE: A low table often placed in front of a sofa.
TABLE TENNIS: A game played with paddles and a small ball on a table.
TENNIS COURT: A marked area where tennis is played.
COURT ORDER: An official instruction from a court.
ORDER FORM: A document used to request goods or services.
FORM LETTER: A standard letter reused for many recipients.
LETTER BOX: A box or slot where letters are delivered.
BOX OFFICE: The place where tickets are sold for a show or event.
BOOK CLUB: A group that reads and discusses books.
CLUB HOUSE: A building used by a club or sports group.
HOUSE PLANT: A plant grown indoors.
PLANT FOOD: Fertilizer or nutrients used to help plants grow.
FOOD TRUCK: A vehicle that sells prepared food.
TRUCK STOP: A roadside place where truck drivers can rest and refuel.
STOP WATCH: A watch used to measure elapsed time.
WATCH BAND: The strap that holds a watch on the wrist.
MUSIC VIDEO: A video made to accompany a song.
VIDEO GAME: An electronic game played on a screen.
GAME SHOW: A television show where contestants play games.
SHOW ROOM: A room where products are displayed for customers.
ROOM KEY: A key used to enter a room, especially in a hotel.
KEY CHAIN: A small chain or ring used to hold keys.
CHAIN REACTION: A series of events where each event causes the next.
REACTION TIME: The time it takes to respond to something.
AIR PORT: A place where airplanes take off and land.
PORT CITY: A city with a harbor or major shipping port.
CITY HALL: The main office building for a city government.
HALL PASS: Permission for a student to leave class or move through a hall.
PASS WORD: A secret word or phrase used to access something.
WORD SEARCH: A puzzle where hidden words are found in a grid of letters.
SEARCH ENGINE: A tool used to find information online.
ENGINE ROOM: The room on a ship where engines are located.
BASE BALL: A bat-and-ball sport played between two teams.
BALL PARK: A field or stadium where baseball is played.
PARK BENCH: A bench found in a park.
BENCH PRESS: A weightlifting exercise done while lying on a bench.
PRESS RELEASE: An official statement sent to the media.
RELEASE DATE: The date when something becomes available.
DATE NIGHT: An evening planned for a romantic outing.
NIGHT OWL: A person who likes staying up late.
RAIN COAT: A waterproof coat worn in rainy weather.
COAT CHECK: A place where coats are stored temporarily.
CHECK LIST: A list used to make sure tasks or items are completed.
LIST PRICE: The official or advertised price of an item.
PRICE TAG: A label that shows the price of something.
TAG TEAM: A team whose members take turns, especially in wrestling.
TEAM PLAYER: Someone who works well with others.
PLAYER PIANO: A piano that can play music automatically.
GARDEN HOSE: A flexible tube used to water a garden.
HOSE PIPE: A hose used for carrying water.
PIPE CLEANER: A flexible wire brush used to clean pipes or for crafts.
CLEANER FISH: A fish that removes parasites or dead skin from other fish.
FISH TANK: A glass container where fish are kept.
TANK TOP: A sleeveless shirt.
TOP SOIL: The upper layer of soil where many plants grow.
SOIL SAMPLE: A small amount of soil taken for testing.
DOG PARK: A park or fenced area where dogs can play.
PARK RANGER: A person who protects and manages a park.
RANGER STATION: A building used by park rangers.
STATION WAGON: A car with a long body and rear storage space.
WAGON WHEEL: A wheel used on a wagon.
WHEEL CHAIR: A chair with wheels used by someone who has difficulty walking.
CHAIR COVER: A cover placed over a chair.
COVER LETTER: A letter sent with a job application or document.
PHONE CALL: A conversation by telephone.
CALL LOG: A record of phone calls.
LOG CABIN: A small house built from logs.
CABIN FEVER: Restlessness from being indoors or isolated too long.
FEVER DREAM: A strange or intense dream, often associated with illness.
DREAM HOUSE: A house that someone would love to live in.
HOUSE PARTY: A party held at someone's home.
PARTY HAT: A hat worn at a party or celebration.
HOME WORK: Schoolwork done outside class.
WORK SHEET: A paper or digital page of exercises or tasks.
SHEET MUSIC: Printed music notation.
MUSIC BOX: A small box that plays music.
BOX LUNCH: A packed lunch served in a box.
LUNCH BREAK: A break from work or school for lunch.
BREAK ROOM: A room where workers can rest or eat.
ROOM MATE: A person who shares a room or apartment.
TOOTH BRUSH: A brush used to clean teeth.
BRUSH STROKE: A mark made by a paintbrush.
STROKE COUNT: The number of strokes taken, especially in swimming or golf.
COUNT DOWN: A backward count before an event.
DOWN HILL: Sloping downward or becoming easier.
HILL SIDE: The side of a hill.
SIDE WALK: A paved path beside a street.
WALK WAY: A path or passage for walking.
CAT FOOD: Food made for cats.
FOOD BOWL: A bowl used to hold food.
BOWL CUT: A haircut with an even rounded shape.
CUT LINE: A line that marks where something should be cut.
LINE COOK: A cook who works at a station in a restaurant kitchen.
COOK BOOK: A book of recipes.
BOOK STORE: A shop that sells books.
STORE CREDIT: Credit a store gives instead of a cash refund.
SOCIAL MEDIA: Websites and apps where people share content and interact.
MEDIA COVERAGE: News attention given to an event or topic.
COVERAGE AREA: The region where a service or signal is available.
AREA CODE: The number used before a local phone number for a region.
CODE SWITCH: To change language or speaking style depending on context.
SWITCH BOARD: A panel or system used to connect electrical or phone lines.
BOARD ROOM: A room where a company's board meets.
ROOM SERVICE: Hotel service that brings food or drinks to a guest's room.
PRIME TIME: The part of the evening when many people watch television.
TIME ZONE: A region that uses the same standard time.
ZONE DEFENSE: A defensive strategy where players guard areas instead of individuals.
DEFENSE MECHANISM: A mental habit used to protect oneself from stress or anxiety.
MECHANISM DESIGN: A field that studies how to design rules or systems for desired outcomes.
DESIGN TEAM: A group responsible for designing a product or experience.
TEAM WORK: Cooperative effort by members of a group.
WORK LOAD: The amount of work someone has to do.
MIND SET: A person's usual way of thinking.
SET POINT: A target value that a system tries to maintain.
POINT GUARD: A basketball player who directs the team's offense.
GUARD RAIL: A rail that prevents people or vehicles from leaving a path or road.
RAIL ROAD: A system of tracks and trains.
ROAD BLOCK: A barrier placed across a road.
BLOCK PARTY: A neighborhood party held on a street or block.
PARTY LINE: The official position or policy of a political party.
SILVER LINING: A hopeful or positive side of a difficult situation.
LINING FABRIC: Fabric used inside clothing, bags, or curtains.
FABRIC SOFTENER: A product used to make clothes feel softer.
SOFTENER SHEET: A sheet used in a dryer to soften clothes and reduce static.
SHEET METAL: Metal formed into thin flat pieces.
METAL DETECTOR: A device used to find metal objects.
DETECTOR DOG: A trained dog that finds substances such as drugs or explosives.
DOG WHISTLE: A high-pitched whistle used to train or call dogs.
MARKET PLACE: A place or system where goods and services are bought and sold.
PLACE VALUE: The value a digit has because of its position in a number.
VALUE JUDGMENT: An opinion based on beliefs about what is good or important.
JUDGMENT CALL: A decision based on personal judgment rather than a fixed rule.
CALL CENTER: An office where workers handle many phone calls.
CENTER STAGE: The central and most visible position on a stage.
STAGE FRIGHT: Nervousness before or during a public performance.
FRIGHT NIGHT: A night associated with scary events or entertainment.
FIELD TRIP: A school trip to learn outside the classroom.
TRIP WIRE: A wire that triggers something when touched or pulled.
WIRE TRANSFER: An electronic transfer of money.
TRANSFER WINDOW: A limited period when sports players can move between teams.
WINDOW SHOPPING: Looking at goods in stores without necessarily buying them.
SHOPPING CART: A cart used to carry items while shopping.
CART WHEEL: A sideways gymnastic move using hands and feet.
WHEEL HOUSE: An area of strength, comfort, or expertise.
CROSS WORD: A word puzzle with clues and intersecting answers.
WORD PLAY: Clever or playful use of words.
PLAY GROUND: An outdoor area where children play.
GROUND RULE: A basic rule agreed on before an activity.
RULE BOOK: A book that explains the rules of a game or organization.
BOOK MARK: A marker used to keep one's place in a book.
MARK DOWN: A reduction in price.
DOWN PAYMENT: Money paid at the start of a purchase or loan.
SHORT STORY: A brief work of fiction.
STORY TIME: A time when stories are read aloud, often to children.
TIME LIMIT: The maximum amount of time allowed.
LIMIT ORDER: An order to buy or sell at a specific price or better.
ORDER BOOK: A list of buy and sell orders in a market.
BOOK REVIEW: A written opinion or evaluation of a book.
REVIEW COPY: A copy provided so someone can review a work.
COPY EDIT: To edit text for grammar, clarity, and style.
PRESENTATION SLIDE: One page or screen in a presentation.
SLIDE SHOW: A sequence of images or slides shown one after another.
SHOW CASE: A display case or a chance to present something at its best.
CASE STUDY: A detailed example used for learning or analysis.
STUDY GUIDE: Material that helps someone prepare or learn.
GUIDE BOOK: A book with information for travelers or learners.
BOOK SHELF: A shelf used to hold books.
SHELF LIFE: The length of time a product remains usable.
BRAIN STORM: A session or process for generating ideas.
STORM CLOUD: A cloud that may bring a storm.
CLOUD COVER: The amount of sky covered by clouds.
COVER STORY: A main story featured on the cover of a magazine or publication.
STORY BOARD: A sequence of drawings used to plan a film, video, or animation.
BOARD MEETING: A formal meeting of a board of directors.
MEETING PLACE: A place where people arrange to meet.
PLACE MAT: A small mat placed under a plate at a table.
PRIVATE EYE: An informal term for a private detective.
EYE CONTACT: Looking directly into another person's eyes.
CONTACT LENS: A thin lens worn on the eye to correct vision.
LENS FLARE: A bright glare or streak caused by light hitting a camera lens.
FLARE GUN: A gun used to fire a signal flare.
GUN CONTROL: Laws or policies about the sale and use of guns.
CONTROL PANEL: A board or screen used to operate a machine or system.
PANEL DISCUSSION: A discussion among a group of invited speakers.
STOCK MARKET: A market where shares of companies are bought and sold.
MARKET SHARE: The portion of total sales controlled by a company or product.
SHARE PRICE: The price of one share of a company.
PRICE INDEX: A measure that tracks changes in prices over time.
INDEX CARD: A small card used for notes or records.
CARD CATALOG: An organized set of cards listing library materials.
CATALOG ENTRY: A record for an item in a catalog.
ENTRY LEVEL: Suitable for beginners or people starting a role.
QUALITY CONTROL: The process of checking that products or work meet standards.
CONTROL GROUP: A comparison group in an experiment.
GROUP CHAT: A digital conversation with several people.
CHAT ROOM: An online space where people exchange messages.
ROOM TEMPERATURE: The normal temperature of an indoor room.
TEMPERATURE CHECK: A check of someone's or something's temperature.
CHECK POINT: A place where people, vehicles, or progress are checked.
POINT SYSTEM: A method of scoring or measuring using points.
SEARCH WARRANT: A legal document allowing authorities to search a place.
WARRANT OFFICER: A military officer with specialized technical authority.
OFFICER TRAINING: Training for people preparing to become officers.
TRAINING MANUAL: A manual that explains how to train or perform tasks.
MANUAL LABOR: Physical work done by people.
LABOR UNION: An organization that represents workers.
UNION STATION: A main station shared by multiple rail lines or services.
STATION MASTER: A person in charge of a railway station.
PRESS CONFERENCE: A meeting where officials answer questions from journalists.
CONFERENCE CALL: A phone or video call with several participants.
CALL OPTION: A financial contract giving the right to buy an asset at a set price.
OPTION TRADING: Buying and selling options contracts.
TRADING FLOOR: The area where traders buy and sell financial products.
FLOOR MANAGER: A person responsible for operations on a floor or venue.
MANAGER REVIEW: An evaluation conducted by or about a manager.
REVIEW BOARD: A group that examines decisions, cases, or proposals.
COLD CASE: An unsolved case that is no longer actively investigated.
CASE FILE: A collection of documents about a case.
FILE CABINET: A cabinet used to store files.
CABINET MAKER: A person who makes cabinets or fine wooden furniture.
MAKER SPACE: A shared place where people make or repair things.
SPACE RACE: A competition to achieve advances in space exploration.
RACE HORSE: A horse bred or trained for racing.
HORSE POWER: A unit used to measure engine power.
GLASS CEILING: An invisible barrier that limits advancement, especially at work.
CEILING FAN: A fan attached to the ceiling.
FAN BASE: The group of people who support a person, team, or product.
BASE CAMP: A main camp used as a starting point for an expedition.
CAMP FIRE: A fire made outdoors at a campsite.
FIRE SIDE: The area beside a fire.
SIDE TRACK: To distract from the main subject or route.
TRACK RECORD: A history of past performance.
RED HERRING: A clue or detail that distracts from the real issue.
HERRING BONE: A V-shaped pattern used in fabric, flooring, or design.
BONE DRY: Completely dry.
DRY RUN: A practice attempt before the real event.
RUN TIME: The length of time something runs, especially a program or show.
TIME CAPSULE: A container holding objects or messages for the future.
CAPSULE WARDROBE: A small collection of versatile clothing items.
WARDROBE MALFUNCTION: An accidental clothing problem, often in public.
WILD GOOSE: A goose that lives in the wild.
GOOSE CHASE: A useless or hopeless search.
CHASE SCENE: A scene in a film or story involving a chase.
SCENE CHANGE: A change from one scene to another in a performance or story.
CHANGE MANAGEMENT: The process of guiding organizational change.
MANAGEMENT CONSULTANT: A professional who advises organizations on management.
CONSULTANT FEE: The amount paid to a consultant.
FEE STRUCTURE: The way fees are arranged or calculated.
RUBBER STAMP: A stamp made of rubber, or an approval given without real review.
STAMP DUTY: A tax paid on some legal or property transactions.
DUTY FREE: Sold without certain local taxes, often at airports.
FREE FALL: Falling freely under gravity.
FALL GUY: A person blamed for someone else's mistake.
GUY LINE: A rope or cable used to steady a tent, pole, or mast.
LINE JUDGE: An official who judges whether a ball or player crosses a line.
JUDGE ADVOCATE: A military legal officer.
CARBON COPY: A duplicate copy of a document or message.
COPY RIGHT: Legal protection for creative work.
RIGHT ANGLE: An angle of 90 degrees.
ANGLE BRACKET: A bracket shaped like < or >.
BRACKET CREEP: A gradual movement into a higher tax bracket due to inflation or income changes.
CREEP FEED: Feed given to young animals while they still nurse.
FEED BACK: Information given in response to performance or work.
BACK CHANNEL: A private or unofficial communication route.
DARK HORSE: A competitor whose abilities are not well known but who may succeed.
HORSE RACE: A race between horses.
RACE CARD: A list of races and runners at a racing event.
CARD SHARP: A skilled card player, often one who wins by trickery.
SHARP TURN: A sudden or tight turn.
TURN SIGNAL: A vehicle light that shows a driver intends to turn.
SIGNAL PROCESSING: The analysis or transformation of signals such as sound or data.
PROCESSING UNIT: A hardware unit that processes data.
SILVER SPOON: A symbol of being born into wealth or privilege.
SPOON FED: Given help or information in an overly easy way.
FED UP: Annoyed, tired, or frustrated with something.
UP SCALE: Expensive or intended for wealthy customers.
SCALE MODEL: A smaller or larger model made in proportion to the real thing.
MODEL CITIZEN: A person considered an excellent member of society.
CITIZEN SCIENCE: Scientific work done with help from the general public.
SCIENCE FICTION: Stories about imagined science, technology, or future worlds.
PAPER TRAIL: Written or printed evidence of actions or decisions.
TRAIL MIX: A snack mixture often eaten while hiking.
MIX TAPE: A collection of songs recorded or arranged together.
TAPE DECK: A device used to play or record cassette tapes.
DECK CHAIR: A folding chair used outdoors.
CHAIR LIFT: A moving seat system that carries people up a slope.
LIFT TICKET: A ticket that allows someone to use ski lifts.
TICKET STUB: The part of a ticket kept after entry.
SWEET TOOTH: A strong liking for sweet foods.
TOOTH PASTE: A paste used to clean teeth.
PASTE BOARD: Thick paperboard made from layers of pasted paper.
BOARD GAME: A game played on a board with pieces or cards.
GAME THEORY: The study of strategic decision-making.
THEORY TEST: A test of theoretical knowledge, often before a practical exam.
TEST CASE: A specific case used to test a rule, system, or idea.
CASE LAW: Law based on previous court decisions.
FALSE START: An invalid start before the proper signal.
START LINE: The line where a race begins.
LINE ITEM: A single entry in a list, budget, or invoice.
ITEM NUMBER: A number used to identify a product or item.
NUMBER CRUNCH: To process or calculate many numbers.
CRUNCH TIME: A short period when intense effort is needed before a deadline.
TIME LAG: A delay between an action and its effect.
LAG SCREW: A large screw used for heavy fastening.
MORAL HAZARD: A situation where someone takes more risk because another party bears the cost.
HAZARD LIGHT: A flashing warning light on a vehicle.
LIGHT POLLUTION: Excess artificial light that affects the night sky or environment.
POLLUTION CONTROL: Measures used to reduce pollution.
CONTROL FREAK: A person who strongly wants to control situations or people.
FREAK SHOW: A display or event presented as strange or sensational.
SHOW TRIAL: A trial held mainly for public display rather than justice.
TRIAL LAWYER: A lawyer who represents clients in court trials.
LOSS LEADER: A product sold cheaply to attract customers.
LEADER BOARD: A ranking list of top scores or performers.
BOARD MEMBER: A person who serves on a board.
MEMBER STATE: A country or state that belongs to an organization.
STATE CAPITAL: The capital city of a state.
CAPITAL LETTER: An uppercase letter.
LETTER HEAD: The printed heading on official stationery.
HEAD COUNT: A count of people present.
RISK PREMIUM: Extra expected return for taking on additional risk.
PREMIUM BOND: A bond sold above its face value.
BOND MARKET: The market where bonds are bought and sold.
MARKET TIMING: Trying to buy or sell based on predicted market movements.
TIMING BELT: A belt that synchronizes parts of an engine.
BELT LOOP: A loop on clothing that holds a belt.
LOOP HOLE: A gap or ambiguity in a rule or law.
HOLE PUNCH: A tool used to make holes in paper.
PUPPET STATE: A country controlled by another power.
STATE SECRET: Information kept secret by a government.
SECRET SERVICE: A government agency responsible for protection or intelligence.
SERVICE INDUSTRY: Businesses that provide services rather than goods.
INDUSTRY STANDARD: A widely accepted standard in a field.
STANDARD ISSUE: Equipment normally provided to members of an organization.
ISSUE BRIEF: A short document explaining a policy issue.
BRIEF CASE: A flat case used for carrying documents.
